---
title: "What Is a Vector, Really?"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"    # data points / vectors
ARROW  = "#c1121f"    # the highlighted arrow
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the front door to the entire **Geometry of
> Data** track and assumes nothing above the Calculus-I floor — the Cartesian plane,
> ordered pairs, and the idea of a function. It does not lean on any other Core Sample.
> Everything the track later builds — span, projection, matrices as maps, the SVD,
> gradients — rests on one idea fixed here: *a vector is a point in space, a list of
> numbers, and a row of a dataset, and those are the same object.* It sets up
> [GEOM-01 *Adding and Scaling*] (the two operations you may perform on the object defined
> here) and the framing the whole track runs on: **a dataset is a cloud of points in a
> room with as many walls as it has features.**

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A vector wears three faces. To a physicist it is an **arrow**: a magnitude and a
direction, floating in space. To a programmer it is a **list of numbers**, a tuple you
can index. To a data scientist it is a **row of a table**: one customer, one patient, one
photograph, recorded as its measured features. This article's single claim is that *these
are not three things that happen to share a name — they are one object seen from three
angles*, and the dictionary between them is the simplest idea in all of linear algebra:
**a coordinate is "how far along each axis."** We build that dictionary carefully, fix the
notation the rest of the track will use, and then take the one leap that makes the
geometry of data possible: a row with 100 columns is a single point in a 100-dimensional
space, and we reason about it with the *exact same rules* as a dot on a page — even though
no one can draw it.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A **vector** is an ordered list of numbers, $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$. That
single list *is*, simultaneously and with no contradiction: a **point** in
$n$-dimensional space (its coordinates are the entries), an **arrow** from the origin to
that point (its direction and length are fixed by the entries), and **one row of a data
table** (each entry is one feature of one observation). The whole of this track is the
study of what you can do to such lists, always asked geometrically: *where is this in
space, and what is being done to the cloud?*
:::

Everything in this article — and, in a real sense, everything in the track — orbits that
box. The payoff is conceptual, not computational: once you genuinely believe that a row of
a spreadsheet is a point in a room, every later tool (measuring the angle between two
customers, projecting a data cloud onto its dominant direction, asking what a matrix
*does* to space) becomes a question about geometry you already have intuition for, just
lifted into more dimensions than you can see.

# Why we are here: the problem that forced the idea {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why would anyone insist that a list of
numbers and an arrow in space are the same thing?** The answer is the entire reason this
track exists, so it is worth telling as the story it actually was.

## Two traditions that did not know they were the same

For most of mathematical history, "arrow" and "list of numbers" lived in different worlds
and different centuries.

The **arrow** came first, from physics and geometry. A force has a size and a direction; so
does a velocity, a displacement, a magnetic field. By the early 1800s, mathematicians like
Bolzano, Möbius, and later Hamilton and Grassmann were formalizing how such directed
quantities add (place them tip-to-tail) and scale (stretch them). This was *geometry*:
pictures of arrows in a plane or in space, with no spreadsheet in sight.

The **list of numbers** came from a different need: solving systems of equations. When you
write down

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x_1 + 2x_2 &= 7,\\
x_1 - 4x_2 &= 1,
\end{aligned}
$$

the *coefficients* $(3,2)$ and $(1,-4)$ and the unknowns $(x_1,x_2)$ are just ordered
tuples of numbers. Through the 19th century this became the algebra of matrices and
determinants (Cayley, Sylvester) — pure symbol manipulation, with no arrows drawn.

The unification — the recognition that *these are the same structure*, an abstract **vector
space**, whether the objects are arrows, tuples, polynomials, or functions — was the work
of Peano (1888) and was made standard by Banach and others in the early 20th century. The
moment it clicked, two enormous toolkits merged: you could now solve a system of equations
by **drawing a picture**, and you could compute on a geometric object by **manipulating its
list of numbers**. Geometry got coordinates; algebra got pictures.

## Why a data scientist should care about a 19th-century merger

Here is the part that makes the *why* concrete for this track, and it is the third face.
A modern dataset is a table. Each row is one observation — one house, one transaction, one
genome — and each column is one measured feature. Consider three houses recorded by area
(in hundreds of square feet) and number of bedrooms:

| house | area (100 sq ft) | bedrooms |
|------:|-----------------:|---------:|
| A     | 12               | 2        |
| B     | 22               | 4        |
| C     | 15               | 3        |

House A is the row $(12, 2)$. By the merger above, that row is *also a point*: go $12$
units along an "area" axis and $2$ along a "bedrooms" axis, and you have plotted house A
in a plane whose two directions are the two features. Three houses become three points —
a tiny **cloud**. And now the entire geometric toolkit is available to ask *data*
questions: Which two houses are most alike? (The two points that are closest, or whose
arrows point most nearly the same way.) Is one feature redundant given another? (Do the
points fall on a line?) What is the "typical" house? (The center of the cloud.)

That is the whole game, and it is why this track is called *Geometry of Data*. **The merger
of arrow and list is what lets us treat a spreadsheet as a shape and bring geometry to
bear on it.** Every later article is one more geometric question asked of the cloud. We
hold onto this *why* throughout: when a definition below looks like dry bookkeeping, it is
there to keep the dictionary between the three faces exact.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Isn't a vector just an array? Why dress it up?"* An interviewer asking this wants to hear
that you know the difference between a **data structure** and a **mathematical object**. A
C array or a NumPy array is the storage; a vector is what that storage *means* the moment
you grant it two operations — addition and scaling (the subject of [GEOM-01]) — and a
geometric home, a space it lives in. The array `[12, 2]` is inert. Calling it a vector
commits you to a geometry: it is a point at coordinates $(12,2)$, an arrow of a definite
length and direction, and a comparison can now be made to other vectors by distance and
angle. The "dressing up" is exactly the content — it is what turns bytes into a place in a
room you can reason about.
:::

# Intuition first: the three faces, before any formalism {#sec-intuition}

Let us *see* the three faces as one object before we write a single definition, because the
picture is the thing to hold onto.

## Face one — the arrow

Pick a starting point, the **origin** $O$, and draw an arrow from it to some destination.
The arrow has a **length** (how far) and a **direction** (which way). That is the
physicist's vector: a directed quantity. Crucially, what pins the arrow down completely is
just *where its tip lands relative to the origin* — and "where the tip lands" is given by
two numbers in the plane: how far right, how far up.

## Face two — the point and its coordinates

Those two numbers are the **coordinates**. Set up two perpendicular axes through the
origin. Then "$3$ units right, $2$ units up" names a single point, and we write it
$(3, 2)$. The first slot is always the first axis, the second slot the second axis: the
list is **ordered**, which is why $(3,2)$ and $(2,3)$ are different points. The tip of the
arrow and the point $(3,2)$ are *literally the same dot on the page* — which is the first
half of the merger. @fig-three-faces draws the arrow and its tip together.

## Face three — the row of data

Now relabel the axes. Instead of "right" and "up," call the first axis *area* and the
second *bedrooms*. The point $(12, 2)$ is no longer abstract — it is **house A**, and its
two coordinates are its two feature values. Nothing about the math changed; we only renamed
the directions. **A data row is a point whose axes are the features.** That is the second
half of the merger, and the foundation of the entire track.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-faces
#| fig-cap: "One object, three faces. **Left:** the vector $\\mathbf{x}=(3,2)$ as an *arrow* from the origin to a tip, and as the *point* where the tip lands — the same dot. The coordinates are read off as 'how far along each axis': $3$ rightward, $2$ upward (dashed). **Right:** relabel the very same axes as data features (area, bedrooms) and the three rows of the house table become three points — a small cloud. No mathematics changed between the panels; only the names on the axes did."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.0))

# --- Left: arrow = point = coordinates ---
axL.annotate("", xy=(3, 2), xytext=(0, 0),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.4))
axL.scatter([3], [2], color=POINT, s=70, zorder=5)
axL.plot([3, 3], [0, 2], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
axL.plot([0, 3], [2, 2], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
axL.text(3.05, 2.12, r"$\mathbf{x}=(3,2)$", color=POINT, fontsize=11)
axL.text(1.5, -0.45, "$3$ along axis 1", color=GREY, ha="center", fontsize=9)
axL.text(-0.15, 1.0, "$2$ along axis 2", color=GREY, ha="right", va="center",
         rotation=90, fontsize=9)
axL.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=30, zorder=5)
axL.text(0.05, -0.35, "origin $O$", fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlim(-0.5, 4.2); axL.set_ylim(-0.8, 3.0)
axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_xlabel("axis 1"); axL.set_ylabel("axis 2")
axL.set_title("Arrow = point = ordered pair")

# --- Right: same plane, axes renamed to features ---
houses = {"A": (12, 2), "B": (22, 4), "C": (15, 3)}
for name, (a, b) in houses.items():
    axR.annotate("", xy=(a, b), xytext=(0, 0),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GREY, lw=1.2, alpha=0.6))
    axR.scatter([a], [b], color=POINT, s=70, zorder=5)
    axR.text(a + 0.4, b + 0.06, f"house {name}", fontsize=9, color=POINT)
axR.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=30, zorder=5)
axR.set_xlim(-1, 26); axR.set_ylim(-0.4, 5)
axR.set_xlabel("area (100 sq ft)"); axR.set_ylabel("bedrooms")
axR.set_title("Same plane, axes are features")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between a point and a vector? Aren't you using them
interchangeably?"* You are, and you should be able to say *why that is legitimate here.*
Strictly, a **point** is a location and a **vector** is a displacement (a "how to get from
here to there"). The two coincide the instant you fix an origin: the point $P$ corresponds
to the unique arrow from $O$ to $P$, and that arrow corresponds back to the point $P$. So
once the origin is chosen — which we always do — "the point $(3,2)$" and "the arrow to
$(3,2)$" are interchangeable names for the same object, and we will switch between them
freely. The honest caveat: this identification is *origin-dependent*. Move the origin and
the same physical arrow gets new coordinates. We will lean on a fixed origin throughout
the track; just know that the freedom to relocate it is real and is what *affine* geometry
studies. For data, the natural origin is often the **center of the cloud** (the mean),
which is why "centering" data — subtracting the mean — is the first move in so many
methods.
:::

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

We now make the three faces precise with as little machinery as the job allows. Define
every symbol on first use; nothing is assumed beyond ordered pairs and the real number
line.

## The space the vectors live in {#sec-rn}

::: {#def-rn}
**Definition (real coordinate space).** For a positive integer $n$, the set
$\mathbb{R}^n$ is the collection of all ordered lists of $n$ real numbers:
$$
\mathbb{R}^n
= \bigl\{\, (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \;:\; x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \in \mathbb{R} \,\bigr\}.
$$
An element $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a **vector**; the real
number $x_i$ is its **$i$-th component** or **$i$-th coordinate**. The integer $n$ is the
**dimension** of the space — the number of slots, equivalently the number of independent
directions, equivalently the number of features in a data row.
:::

Three pieces of this definition deserve unpacking, because each carries one of the three
faces.

- **"$\mathbb{R}$"** is the set of real numbers — the entire number line, including
  negatives, fractions, and irrationals. The boldface $\mathbb{R}$ (a "blackboard" $R$) is
  standard for it. The superscript $n$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ means "an ordered list of $n$ of
  them," exactly as $\mathbb{R}^2$ is the familiar coordinate *plane* and $\mathbb{R}^3$ is
  three-dimensional *space*. So $\mathbb{R}^n$ is just "the plane, but with $n$ axes instead
  of $2$." We will retire the picture but keep the rules.

- **"Ordered."** The list $(3, 2)$ is not the list $(2, 3)$: the first slot belongs to the
  first axis (or first feature) and the second to the second. Order is the whole reason a
  vector can encode *which* feature is *which*. This is the precise sense in which a vector
  differs from a mere *set* of numbers, where order would not matter.

- **Notation we fix now and keep for the track.** Vectors are lowercase boldface,
  $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{v}$; their components are the same letter, not bold, with
  a subscript index, $x_1, x_2, \dots$. Plain (non-bold) lowercase letters like $c$ or
  $\lambda$ will be **scalars** — single real numbers, the things we scale vectors by in
  [GEOM-01]. When a component itself needs a second index — as it will once we stack many
  data rows — we write $x_{ij}$ for "row $i$, feature $j$," and we will flag that the moment
  it appears.

## Column vectors, and why we prefer them {#sec-column}

We have been writing vectors *across* the page, $(x_1, \dots, x_n)$, because it reads like
a list. From here on, the track's working form is the **column**:

$$
\mathbf{x} =
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-column}

This is the same vector — same components, same order, same point in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — only
stood up vertically. The reason for the preference is not aesthetics: when matrices arrive
([GEOM-08]) and act on vectors by the rule "matrix times column," the column shape is what
makes the multiplication line up. We adopt it now so no renotation is needed later. The
horizontal form $(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ remains a convenient way to *write* a column inline,
and the operation that formally turns one into the other — the **transpose**, written
$\mathbf{x}^\top$ — gets its full geometric meaning in [GEOM-10]; here it is just "lay the
column on its side."

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Row vector or column vector — does it matter?"* For storing a single observation, no: it
is the same $n$ numbers either way. It starts to matter the instant you multiply by a
matrix, because matrix multiplication is shape-sensitive (it pairs *rows of the left* with
*columns of the right*, derived in [GEOM-09]). The near-universal convention in this track,
and in most of statistics and machine learning, is: **a vector is a column by default**,
and a **data table stacks one observation per row** — so the matrix $X$ of a dataset has
each *row* equal to one observation written across. Those two conventions seem to fight,
and reconciling them (an observation is a column vector, but it sits as a row of $X$) is a
classic source of indexing bugs. The fix is to keep one picture in mind: $X$'s rows are
observations, $X$'s columns are features, and a lone observation pulled out of $X$ is, by
default, a column.
:::

## The two special vectors {#sec-special}

Two vectors will recur so often they earn names now.

::: {#def-zero}
**Definition (zero vector).** The **zero vector** $\mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the
vector whose every component is $0$:
$$
\mathbf{0} = (0, 0, \dots, 0).
$$
:::

Geometrically $\mathbf{0}$ is the **origin** — the point all our arrows start from, the
one location that is its own arrow (an arrow of zero length, pointing nowhere). It is the
unique vector with no direction and zero length. In the data picture, the origin is the
"all features equal to zero" observation, which is why centering a dataset (subtracting the
mean from every feature) deliberately *moves the cloud so its center sits at* $\mathbf{0}$
— it puts the interesting geometry around the origin where the algebra is cleanest.

The second family is the **standard basis vectors**, the unit steps along each axis. In
$\mathbb{R}^n$ there are $n$ of them, $\mathbf{e}_1, \dots, \mathbf{e}_n$, where
$\mathbf{e}_i$ has a $1$ in slot $i$ and $0$ everywhere else. In $\mathbb{R}^3$:

$$
\mathbf{e}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{e}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{e}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-basis}

These are exactly the axes themselves, turned into vectors: $\mathbf{e}_1$ is "one unit
along the first axis." They make precise the phrase from the Core — *a coordinate is "how
far along each axis"* — because, as the next subsection shows, every vector is its
coordinates measured against these axis-vectors. We only name them here; what they truly
*are* (a minimal set of directions that builds everything, a **basis**) is the subject of
[GEOM-03].

## "How far along each axis," made exact {#sec-coords}

The single sentence that powers the whole article is *a coordinate is how far along each
axis*. We can now state it without hand-waving, using only the names just defined. Take any
$\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$. Then

$$
\mathbf{x}
= \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ x_2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
= x_1\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
+ x_2\!\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
+ x_3\!\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
= x_1\mathbf{e}_1 + x_2\mathbf{e}_2 + x_3\mathbf{e}_3.
$$ {#eq-coords}

Let us justify each equals-sign so nothing is taken on faith.

- The **first** equality is just @eq-column: the column form of $\mathbf{x}$.
- The **second** equality splits the single column into a sum of three columns, one
  carrying each component and zeros elsewhere. This is legitimate because adding vectors is
  defined slot-by-slot — the entry in slot $1$ of the sum is $x_1 + 0 + 0 = x_1$, and
  likewise for the others, so the right side really does reassemble $\mathbf{x}$. (We are
  *using* componentwise addition here; it is *defined and drawn* as the first operation of
  [GEOM-01], so this is a forward pointer, not a hidden assumption — the arithmetic
  $x_1+0+0=x_1$ is all that is needed and it is plain.)
- The **third** equality pulls the lone nonzero number out of each column. A column with
  $x_1$ on top and zeros below is $x_1$ copies of the column with $1$ on top and zeros below
  — that is what scaling a vector by a number does, again slot-by-slot
  ($x_1 \cdot 1 = x_1$, $x_1 \cdot 0 = 0$). This scaling operation is the *second* operation
  formalized in [GEOM-01]; here we need only the grade-school fact that $x_1 \cdot 0 = 0$.
- The **fourth** equality is the definition @eq-basis of the $\mathbf{e}_i$, read backwards.

The result @eq-coords is worth saying in words, because it is the precise form of the Core's
slogan: **every vector is built by walking $x_1$ steps along the first axis, $x_2$ along the
second, $x_3$ along the third — and the coordinates are exactly the number of steps.** The
same statement holds in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with $n$ terms,
$\mathbf{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \mathbf{e}_i$. The expression on the right — a sum of scaled
vectors — is the **linear combination**, the one move from which the rest of linear algebra
is built; [GEOM-01] is devoted to it. We have just shown that *the coordinates of a vector
are the recipe for rebuilding it from the axes*, which is why "how far along each axis" is
not a metaphor but a literal decomposition.

# The leap: dimensions you cannot draw {#sec-leap}

Here is the move that the whole track depends on and that beginners find unsettling, so we
make it slowly and honestly.

## What changes past three dimensions, and what does not

In @def-rn we let $n$ be *any* positive integer. For $n = 1, 2, 3$ we can draw the space: a
line, a plane, a room. For $n = 4$ and beyond, **we cannot draw it, and we cannot picture
it, and that is fine** — because nothing in @def-rn, @eq-column, or @eq-coords ever required
a picture. A vector in $\mathbb{R}^{100}$ is a list of $100$ real numbers. The zero vector
is $100$ zeros. The point is "$x_1$ along axis $1$, …, $x_{100}$ along axis $100$." Every
rule we have written is *componentwise* — it speaks of slot $1$, slot $2$, and so on — and
componentwise rules do not care how many slots there are. **The algebra is identical; only
the picture fails.**

This is the leap: a row of a data table with $100$ features *is* a single point in
$\mathbb{R}^{100}$, and we reason about it with the same dictionary we used for the house in
the plane. "Which two observations are most alike" is still "which two points are closest";
we just measure closeness with a formula (the distance, built in [GEOM-04]) instead of a
ruler. The geometry survives into dimensions we cannot see, carried entirely by the
coordinates.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How can you talk about a 100-dimensional space if you can't visualize it?"* The honest,
confident answer: *we reason with the coordinate definition, not the picture.* The picture
is a crutch that happens to work in $n \le 3$; the *definition* — an ordered list of $n$
reals, with everything done slot-by-slot — works for every $n$ and is what we actually
compute with. We *port intuition up* from the cases we can see (closeness, direction, "lies
on a flat sheet through the origin") and *verify it with the algebra*, which is dimension-
blind. The mature stance is: visualize in 2-D or 3-D to *generate* a conjecture, then trust
the coordinates to *settle* it. The companion warning is below — some of that ported
intuition does break, and a good practitioner knows which.
:::

## An honest worked example: text as vectors, near the edge {#sec-edge}

To make the leap concrete *and* to show where the geometric intuition starts to strain, we
turn three short documents into vectors and look at the cloud — including the part of the
picture that lies past where we can draw.

A standard, genuinely-used encoding is the **bag of words**: fix a vocabulary, and let each
document become a vector whose $j$-th component is the count of vocabulary word $j$ in that
document. The space has one axis per vocabulary word — easily thousands of dimensions for
real text. We keep a five-word vocabulary so the *vectors* are honest but small.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-docs
#| echo: false
import numpy as np

vocab = ["model", "data", "loss", "cat", "nap"]
docs = {
    "D1 (ML note)":   "the model fits the data, the model lowers the loss on data",
    "D2 (ML note)":   "data drives the model; loss measures model error on the data",
    "D3 (cat blog)":  "the cat took a nap, then the cat wanted another nap",
}

def bag(text, vocab):
    words = text.lower().replace(",", " ").replace(";", " ").split()
    return np.array([sum(w == v for w in words) for v in vocab], dtype=int)

vecs = {name: bag(t, vocab) for name, t in docs.items()}

print("vocabulary axes:", vocab)
print()
for name, v in vecs.items():
    print(f"{name:14s} -> {v.tolist()}")
```

Each document is now a point in $\mathbb{R}^5$ — a five-feature row, exactly like the house
but with word-counts for features. We cannot draw $\mathbb{R}^5$. What we *can* do is the
geometry that does not need a picture: compare directions. Two documents about the same
topic should point roughly the *same way* (high counts on the same axes), even if one is
longer (a bigger arrow). The figure below shows the count vectors as a heat-style bar
picture (what we *can* render) alongside a 2-D shadow of the cloud (a deliberate
compression of the 5-D points down to a plane, so we can place a flat picture next to the
honest 5-D numbers).

```{python}
#| label: fig-docs
#| fig-cap: "Three documents as vectors in $\\mathbb{R}^5$, one axis per vocabulary word. **Left:** the honest object — each document's five word-counts, the thing that actually lives in 5-D. The two machine-learning notes (D1, D2) load on the same axes (`model`, `data`, `loss`); the cat blog (D3) loads on entirely different ones (`cat`, `nap`). **Right:** a 2-D *shadow* of the same three points (a projection, the operation of [GEOM-06]); D1 and D2 land near each other and D3 far off, which is the cloud's real structure — but the shadow is lossy, a point we make honest in the text."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.0))

names = list(vecs.keys())
M = np.array([vecs[n] for n in names], dtype=float)  # 3 x 5

# --- Left: the honest 5-D objects as grouped bars ---
width = 0.25
xpos = np.arange(len(vocab))
colors = [POINT, ACCENT, ARROW]
for k, n in enumerate(names):
    axL.bar(xpos + (k - 1) * width, M[k], width, label=n, color=colors[k], alpha=0.9)
axL.set_xticks(xpos); axL.set_xticklabels(vocab, rotation=0)
axL.set_ylabel("word count (coordinate)")
axL.set_title("Each document = 5 coordinates")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

# --- Right: a 2-D shadow via the top-2 principal directions (just for a picture) ---
Mc = M - M.mean(axis=0, keepdims=True)
U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(Mc, full_matrices=False)
coords2d = Mc @ Vt[:2].T
for k, n in enumerate(names):
    axR.scatter(coords2d[k, 0], coords2d[k, 1], color=colors[k], s=110, zorder=5)
    axR.annotate(n, (coords2d[k, 0], coords2d[k, 1]),
                 textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, 4), fontsize=9, color=colors[k])
axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8); axR.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8)
axR.set_xlabel("shadow direction 1"); axR.set_ylabel("shadow direction 2")
axR.set_title("A lossy 2-D shadow of the 5-D cloud")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Read the figure for the lesson it teaches, including the uncomfortable part. The left panel
is the *truth*: three points in five dimensions, and you can verify by eye that D1 and D2
have their weight on the same three axes while D3 sits on the other two. The right panel is
a *shadow* — we squashed five dimensions down to two so a flat picture could exist — and it
shows D1 and D2 near each other, D3 banished to the side, which faithfully reflects the
real structure. **But the shadow is not the object.** Two points that look close in the
2-D shadow could be far apart in the true $\mathbb{R}^5$, because the squashing can hide
disagreement along the three axes it threw away. This is the honest edge of "just visualize
it": **every low-dimensional picture of high-dimensional data is a projection, and
projection loses information.** The right tool — how to choose the *least* lossy shadow,
and how to quantify what it discarded — is the projection of [GEOM-06] and the SVD of
[GEOM-20], built later in this track. For now the takeaway is exactly the article's thesis,
with its caveat attached: the row *is* a point in $\mathbb{R}^5$; we may *draw* only its
shadow; and we must not confuse the drawing with the thing.

## Where ported intuition genuinely breaks {#sec-curse}

Some intuition from the plane survives the leap (closeness, direction, lines and flats
through the origin). Some does not, and being unaware of the difference is how people get
burned. The sharpest example, which the track returns to with full machinery later, is that
**in high dimensions almost everything is far from almost everything, and roughly the same
distance away.** Pack points into $\mathbb{R}^2$ and some are clearly near, others clearly
far. Do the same in $\mathbb{R}^{1000}$ and the *spread* of pairwise distances shrinks
relative to their *typical* value — the comfortable 2-D notion that "there are close
neighbors and far strangers" weakens. This is the so-called **curse of dimensionality**,
and it is why nearest-neighbor methods that feel obviously sensible in the plane can quietly
fail on wide data.

```{python}
#| label: fig-curse
#| fig-cap: "An honest edge case: intuition that does *not* survive the leap. For dimensions $n = 2, 10, 100, 1000$ we drop $500$ random points uniformly in the unit cube of $\\mathbb{R}^n$ and measure all pairwise distances. **Left (raw distances):** as $n$ grows the whole distribution marches rightward — *everything* is genuinely farther apart, because each new axis adds another nonnegative squared gap under the root. **Right (each distribution divided by its own mean, so all are centered at $1$):** the *relative* spread collapses — the cloud of distances tightens around its typical value, so the gap between the nearest and farthest neighbor of a point shrinks toward nothing. The printed coefficient of variation (standard deviation $\\div$ mean) falls from $\\approx 0.48$ at $n=2$ to $\\approx 0.05$ at $n=1000$. That collapse — not the rightward march — is the curse: 'there are close neighbors and far strangers' stops being true."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0))
dims = [2, 10, 100, 1000]
shades = ["#cfe3f5", "#7fb3e0", "#3d7fc0", "#13325c"]
print("dimension |  mean dist |  std dist |  CV = std/mean")
for d, c in zip(dims, shades):
    P = rng.random((500, d))
    # pairwise distances among a sample of point-pairs
    idx = rng.choice(500, size=(4000, 2))
    diffs = P[idx[:, 0]] - P[idx[:, 1]]
    dists = np.sqrt(np.sum(diffs ** 2, axis=1))
    mu, sd = dists.mean(), dists.std()
    print(f"n = {d:5d} | {mu:10.3f} | {sd:9.3f} | {sd / mu:13.3f}")
    # Left: raw distances -> the rightward march (everything farther apart)
    axL.hist(dists, bins=40, density=True, histtype="stepfilled",
             alpha=0.6, color=c, label=f"$n={d}$")
    # Right: divide each distribution by its OWN mean -> relative spread only
    axR.hist(dists / mu, bins=40, density=True, histtype="stepfilled",
             alpha=0.6, color=c, label=f"$n={d}$")
axL.set_xlabel("raw pairwise distance")
axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Everything moves farther apart")
axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)
axR.axvline(1.0, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.0)
axR.set_xlabel("pairwise distance / (its own mean)")
axR.set_xlim(0, 2)
axR.set_title("...but the spread around the mean collapses")
axR.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
```

We do not resolve the curse here — that needs the dot product and norms ([GEOM-04]) to even
state precisely, and it ramifies through Classification and Unsupervised Learning. We flag
it now for one reason consistent with this article's job: **the leap into high dimensions is
real and powerful, but it is not free.** The coordinate definition ports perfectly; *some*
geometric instincts do not. Knowing which is the beginning of practitioner judgment, and the
rest of the track is partly an inventory of exactly that.

# Failure modes and honest boundaries {#sec-failure}

This article defines an object rather than a method, so its "failure modes" are the
*misreadings* of the object — the places where calling something a vector commits you to
more (or less) than you intended.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Not every list of numbers should be treated as a vector.** The definition is free — any
$n$ reals form an element of $\mathbb{R}^n$ — but the *geometry* you inherit (distances,
angles, "averaging two points") is only meaningful if the components are commensurable
quantities that it makes sense to add and scale. Three traps:

- **Categorical codes.** If you encode `{red, green, blue}` as `{1, 2, 3}`, the vector
  arithmetic will happily compute that "green is the average of red and blue," which is
  nonsense. The integers are *labels*, not positions on an axis. The fix is one-hot
  encoding — turn the single coded column into three indicator axes — which *does* yield an
  honest vector.
- **Mismatched units and scales.** Area in square feet (values in the thousands) and
  bedrooms (values $1$–$5$) live on wildly different scales, so a raw distance in
  $\mathbb{R}^2$ is dominated by area and effectively ignores bedrooms. The geometry is
  valid but *misleading* until the features are standardized — a recurring theme once we
  have norms ([GEOM-04]).
- **Identifiers.** A ZIP code or a customer ID is stored as a number but has no axis
  meaning at all; subtracting two ZIP codes is meaningless. It is data, but it is not a
  coordinate.

The lesson: *being storable as a list of numbers is necessary but not sufficient for being
a vector you can do geometry to.* The components must be quantities for which "how far
along this axis" is a real statement.
:::

A second boundary worth stating plainly: this article fixes the **standard** axes and the
**standard** notion of $\mathbb{R}^n$. There are vectors that are not tuples of real numbers
at all — polynomials, functions, random variables — which obey the same two operations and
so form vector spaces too. The track deliberately *does not* pursue that abstraction
([GEOM-01] explains the eight rules as "what addition and scaling obey," not as axioms to be
revered), because the data-science payoff lives almost entirely in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Knowing
the abstraction exists is enough; chasing it is out of scope by design.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me three different things that are all vectors."* A crisp answer that shows range:
**(1)** the velocity of a thrown ball — an arrow in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with magnitude (speed)
and direction; **(2)** one row of a customer table — `(age, income, purchases)` as a point
in $\mathbb{R}^3$ whose axes are features; **(3)** a grayscale image — a $28 \times 28$
photo flattened into $784$ pixel-intensity components, a single point in $\mathbb{R}^{784}$.
The interviewer is checking that you see the *unifying structure*: all three are ordered
lists of reals on which addition and scaling make sense, so all three live in some
$\mathbb{R}^n$ and all three submit to the same geometry — even the image, which we can
display but never plot *as a point* in its $784$-dimensional home.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You are at the **start** of the Geometry of Data track. This article fixed the object —
the vector as arrow, as list, as data row — and the dictionary among the three faces, plus
the leap that lets a $100$-feature row be a single point in $\mathbb{R}^{100}$. From here
the build is mostly linear:

- **[GEOM-01 *Adding and Scaling: The Linear Combination*]** — the *only two* operations
  linear algebra grants this object, defined componentwise and drawn geometrically
  (tip-to-tail addition, stretch/flip scaling). Every "we are using componentwise
  addition/scaling" forward-pointer in @sec-coords is discharged there.
- **[GEOM-02 *Span and Subspaces*]** — having one move (the linear combination), we ask
  *which points it can reach* from a given handful of vectors, and what shape that set is.
- **[GEOM-04 *The Dot Product*]** — equips $\mathbb{R}^n$ with **length and angle**, which is
  how we finally measure the "closeness" and "same direction" this article could only
  gesture at, and how the curse of dimensionality ([@sec-curse]) gets stated precisely.
- **[GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection*]** — the keystone, and the honest tool behind the lossy
  2-D *shadow* of [@fig-docs]: it tells us how to cast a high-dimensional cloud down to a
  picture and exactly what gets lost.
- **[GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear Maps*]** — once a dataset is a *stack* of these row-vectors,
  a matrix is the natural object that acts on the whole cloud at once.

The running thread the track never drops: **a dataset is a cloud of points in a room with
as many walls as it has features**, and every later technique is a geometric question asked
of that cloud.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The historical merger.** Giuseppe Peano's 1888 *Calcolo geometrico* contains the first
  modern axioms of a vector space; Hermann Grassmann's 1844 *Ausdehnungslehre* is the
  ambitious, under-read origin of the "arrows you can add and scale" view. The fusion with
  the algebra-of-tuples tradition (Cayley, Sylvester) into today's single notion is a
  20th-century standardization (Banach, and the textbook tradition after him).
- **The leap, taken seriously.** The fact that a $784$-pixel image *is* a point in
  $\mathbb{R}^{784}$ is the working premise of nearly all modern machine learning; the
  honest counterweight — that high-dimensional geometry is genuinely strange — is surveyed
  under "the curse of dimensionality," which we will be equipped to make precise after
  [GEOM-04].
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do we add and scale these
  objects, exactly?* — [GEOM-01]. *How do we measure length, distance, and angle so
  "closeness" and "same direction" become numbers?* — [GEOM-04]. *How do we draw the least-
  lossy flat picture of a high-dimensional cloud, and quantify what the picture threw away?*
  — [GEOM-06] and [GEOM-20]. Each is a direct continuation of a promise made above.
